name: lonboard (Fast Large-Scale Geospatial Visualization)
pricing_notes: Open source (MIT); Development Seed / Kyle Barron

performance.notes: deck.gl WebGL rendering; GeoArrow binary transfer; millions of features supported

agent_readiness.notes: High-performance geospatial visualization in Jupyter via deck.gl WebGL. from lonboard import Map, ScatterplotLayer, PathLayer, SolidPolygonLayer. layer = ScatterplotLayer.from_geopandas(gdf, get_radius=100, get_fill_color=[255, 0, 0]). m = Map(layer). m. GeoArrow binary encoding for fast Python-to-browser transfer. Handles millions of points. PathLayer for lines. SolidPolygonLayer for polygons. Color by column: ColorAccessor. Size by column: FloatAccessor. Requires Jupyter.

gotchas: Jupyter-only — not for standalone HTML maps (use pydeck or kepler.gl)
Limited to ScatterplotLayer, PathLayer, SolidPolygonLayer — no heatmap/hexagon
Color/size accessors require understanding GeoArrow encoding
Newer library — API may change between minor versions
best_when: Visualizing millions of geospatial features in Jupyter without performance issues
GPU-rendered geospatial exploration where folium/leaflet is too slow
Point clouds and large trajectory datasets
When GeoArrow/GeoDataFrame data is already available
avoid_when: Standalone HTML map output needed (use folium or pydeck)
Complex layer types (heatmaps, hexagons — use pydeck)
Small datasets where performance isn't a concern
summary: lonboard provides fast large-scale geospatial visualization in Jupyter via deck.gl WebGL. ScatterplotLayer.from_geopandas(gdf). PathLayer, SolidPolygonLayer. GeoArrow binary transfer for millions of features. MIT by Development Seed/Kyle Barron. Best for large point/line/polygon datasets in Jupyter where leaflet is too slow. Jupyter-only; limited layer types vs full pydeck.
